name: Past Tense (-di)
summary: The witnessed past tense, used for completed actions you know happened: -di and its hard variant -ti.
explanation:
The -di past tense is the everyday way to talk about finished actions you witnessed or know for certain happened. It is called the definite or seen past. Gel- (come) becomes geldi (he came), and yaz- (write) becomes yazdı (he wrote). The personal endings follow.

The suffix harmonises four ways in its vowel (-di, -dı, -du, -dü) and also changes its first consonant. After a voiceless consonant (p, ç, t, k, s, ş, h, f) the d hardens to t, so git- (go) becomes gitti (he went) and iç- (drink) becomes içti (he drank). After voiced sounds it stays -di.

The personal endings are -m (ben), -n (sen), nothing (o), -k (biz), -niz (siz), and -ler (onlar): geldim, geldin, geldi, geldik, geldiniz, geldiler. These same endings, with harmony, attach after every -di form, so once you know one verb the pattern repeats.
commonMistakes: Keeping -di after a voiceless consonant, saying gitdi instead of gitti.
Ignoring vowel harmony, saying geldim fine but yazdim instead of yazdım.
Confusing the witnessed past -di with the reported past -miş, which means 'apparently' happened.
Forgetting the personal ending, saying ben geldi instead of ben geldim.
Adding an extra buffer where none is needed, since -di attaches directly to the stem.
tip: Listen to the last consonant of the stem. If it is one of the eight voiceless ones (think of the word 'fıstıkçı şahap'), the d turns into t: gitti, içti, yaptı.
